en.extract:
Anna's hummingbird (Calypte anna) is a North American species of hummingbird  named after Anna Masséna, Duchess of Rivoli.
It is native to western coastal regions of North America. Until the late 20th century, Anna's hummingbirds migrated from locations as far north as Alaska and coastal British Columbia, returning south to breed in Baja California and Southern California. 
Since the 1970s, ornamental plants in residential areas along the Pacific coast and inland deserts provided expanded nectar and nesting sites, allowing the species to expand its breeding range to northern coastal regions without migrating. Year-round residence of Anna's hummingbirds in the Pacific Northwest is an example of ecological release dependent on acclimation to colder winter temperatures, introduced plants, and human provision of nectar feeders during winter.
These birds feed on nectar from flowers using a long extendable tongue. They also consume small insects and other arthropods caught in flight or gleaned from vegetation.


Taxonomy
Anna's hummingbird was formally described and illustrated in 1829 by the French naturalist René Lesson in his Histoire naturelle des Oiseaux-Mouches from a specimen that had been collected in California. Lesson placed it in the genus Ornismya and coined the binomial name Ornismya anna. Anna's hummingbird is now placed in the genus Calypte that was introduced in 1856 by the English ornithologist John Gould. Gould did not explain the derivation of the genus name but it is probably from the Ancient Greek kaluptrē meaning "woman's veil" or "head-dress" (from kaluptō meaning "to cover"). The specific epithet anna was chosen to honour Anne d'Essling who married the ornithologist François Victor Massena, 3rd Duke of Rivoli. The species is monotypic: no subspecies are recognised.


Description
Anna's hummingbirds are 3.9 to 4.3 in (9.9 to 10.9 cm) long with a wingspan of 4.7 inches (12 cm) and a weight range of 0.1 to 0.2 oz (2.8 to 5.7 g). They have an iridescent bronze-green back, a pale grey chest and belly, and green flanks. Their bills are long, straight, and slender. The adult male has an iridescent crimson-red, derived from magenta, to a reddish-pink crown and gorget, which can look dull brown or gray without direct sunlight, and a dark, slightly forked tail. Females also have iridescent red gorgets, although they are usually smaller and less brilliant than the male.

The male Anna's hummingbird is the only North American hummingbird species with a red crown. Females and juvenile males have a dull green crown, a grey throat with or without some red iridescence, a grey chest and belly, and a dark, rounded tail with white tips on the outer feathers.
The male has a striking reddish-pink crown and gorget, which are strongly iridescent and dependent on the angle of illumination and observation by female or male competitor birds. The iridescence results from large stacks of melanosomes in the feather barbules, occurring as layers separated by keratin. The barbules reflect incident light in the manner of partially-opened Venetian blinds, enabling the iridescence – which varies the head and gorget coloration with the changing angle of light – as a coloration advantage for courtship attraction and territory defense.
Male birds with elevated levels of protein in their diet have more colorful crowns and higher iridescence in their head feathers compared with male birds with low protein intake.
The male's call – scratchy and metallic – is typically used as the bird perches in trees and shrubs. Anna's hummingbirds have adapted to urban environments and are commonly seen in backyards and parks, and at feeders and flowering plants. Anna's hummingbirds eat flying insects.


Population statistics
According to a 2021 estimate, there are 8 million Anna's hummingbirds in the western United States and Canada, with the population increasing since 1970. The global population is estimated to be 9.6 million mature individuals. As of 2021, the International Union for Conservation of Nature Red List of Threatened Species lists Anna's hummingbird among species of least concern.


Distribution and habitat
Anna's hummingbird is found along the western coast of North America, from southern Canada to northern Baja California, and inland to southern and central Arizona, extreme southern Nevada and southeastern Utah, and western Texas. They tend to be permanent residents within their range. However, birds have been spotted far outside their range in such places as southern Alaska, Idaho, Saskatchewan, New York, Florida, Louisiana, and Newfoundland.
In response to rising temperatures at low elevations during climate change in the 21st century,  Anna's hummingbirds have expanded their range into the cooler summer environments of higher-altitude (up to 2,825 metres (9,268 ft)) mountainous terrains of California, such as the Sierra Nevada.


Northern range expansion
Anna's hummingbirds have the northernmost year-round range of any hummingbird. Birds have been recorded in Alaska as early as 1971, and resident in the Pacific Northwest since the 1960s, particularly increasing as a resident population during the early 21st century. Scientists estimate that some birds overwinter and presumably breed at northern latitudes where food and shelter are available throughout winter, tolerating moderately cold winter temperatures.
During cold temperatures, Anna's hummingbirds gradually gain weight during the day as they convert sugar to fat.
While their range was originally limited to the chaparral of California and Baja California, it expanded northward to Oregon, Washington, and British Columbia, and east to Arizona in the 1960s and 70s. This rapid expansion is attributed to the widespread planting of non-native species, such as eucalyptus, as well as the use of urban bird feeders, in combination with the species' natural tendency for extensive postbreeding dispersal.
In the Pacific Northwest, the fastest growing populations occur in regions with breeding-season cold temperatures similar to those of its native range. Northward expansion of Anna's hummingbird represents an ecological release associated with introduced plants, year-round nectar availability from feeders supplied by humans, milder winter temperatures possibly associated with climate change, and acclimation of the species to a winter climate cooler than its native region. Although quantitative data are absent, it is likely that a sizable percentage of Anna's hummingbirds in the Pacific Northwest still do migrate south for winter, as of 2017.


Torpor
Anna's hummingbirds with inadequate stores of body fat or insufficient plumage are able to survive periods of subfreezing weather by lowering their metabolic rate and entering a state of torpor. 
When studied in colder temperatures at mountainous elevations, Anna's hummingbirds used torpor more frequently and for longer periods than at lower elevations.


Behavior


Foraging

This species consumes tree sap, nectar from flowers, insects, and sugar-water mixes from feeders. Anna's hummingbirds capture flying insects or eat insects trapped in spider webs, sometimes even the spiders themselves.


Pollination
While collecting nectar, Anna's hummingbird assists in plant pollination. There is evidence that Anna's hummingbirds in flight generate an electrostatic charge that adheres pollen to their beaks and feathers, facilitating transfer of pollen grains to hundreds of flowers per day while foraging for nectar. 
Anna's hummingbirds can shake their bodies 55 times per second to shed rain while in flight, or in dry weather, to remove pollen or dirt from feathers. Each twist lasts four-hundredths of a second and applies 34 times the force of gravity on the bird's head.


Locomotion
During hovering flight, Anna's hummingbirds maintain high wingbeat frequencies accomplished by their large pectoral muscles via recruitment of motor units. The pectoral muscles that power hummingbird flight are composed exclusively of fast glycolytic fibers that respond rapidly and are fatigue-resistant.


Song and courtship

Unlike most northern temperate hummingbirds, the male Anna's hummingbird sings during courtship. The song is thin and squeaky, interspersed with buzzes and chirps, and is drawn to over 10 seconds in duration. During the breeding season, males can be observed performing an aerial display dive over their territories. When a female flies onto a male's territory, the male rises up about 130 ft (40 m) before diving over the female. As the male approaches the bottom of the dive, it reaches an average speed of 27 m/s (89 ft/s), which is 385 body lengths per second. At the bottom of the dive, the male travels 23 m/s (51 mph), and produces an audible sound produced by the tail feathers, described by some as an "explosive squeak".


Breeding
Open-wooded or shrubby areas and mountain meadows along the Pacific coast from British Columbia to Arizona make up C. anna's breeding habitat. The female raises the young without the assistance of the male. The female bird builds a nest in a shrub or tree, in vines, or attached to wires or other artificial substrates. The round, 3.8-to-5.1-centimetre (1.5 to 2.0 in) diameter nest is constructed of plant fibers, downy feathers and animal hair; the exterior is camouflaged with chips of lichen, plant debris, and occasionally urban detritus such as paint chips and cigarette paper. The nest materials are bound together with spider silk. They are known to nest as early as mid-December and as late as June, depending on geographic location and climatic conditions.


Hybridization
Anna's hummingbirds may hybridize with other species, especially the congeneric Costa's hummingbird. These natural hybrids have been mistaken for new species. A bird, allegedly collected in Bolaños, Mexico, was described and named Selasphorus floresii (Gould, 1861), or Floresi's hummingbird. Several more specimens were collected in California over a long period, and the species was considered extremely rare.
The specimens were the hybrid offspring of an Anna's hummingbird and an Allen's hummingbird. A single bird collected in Santa Barbara, California, was described and named Trochilus violajugulum (Jeffries, 1888), or violet-throated hummingbird. It was later determined to be a hybrid between an Anna's hummingbird and a black-chinned hummingbird.


Urbanization
In the 2017 Vancouver Official City Bird Election, Anna's hummingbird was named the official bird of the city of Vancouver, British Columbia. 
Anna's hummingbirds are non-migrating residents of Seattle where they live year-round through winter, enduring extended periods of subfreezing temperatures, snow, and high winds.


Gallery


References


External links

Anna's hummingbird Species Account – Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Anna's Hummingbird photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

'''Anna's hummingbird''' ('''''Calypte anna''''') is a North American species of [[hummingbird]]  named after [[Anna Masséna, Duchess of Rivoli]].

It is native to western coastal regions of North America. Until the late 20th century, Anna's hummingbirds migrated from locations as far north as [[Alaska]] and coastal [[British Columbia]], returning south to breed in [[Baja California]] and [[Southern California]]. 

Since the 1970s, [[ornamental plant]]s in residential areas along the [[Pacific coast]] and inland deserts provided expanded nectar and [[bird nest|nesting]] sites, allowing the species to expand its breeding range to northern coastal regions without migrating. Year-round residence of Anna's hummingbirds in the [[Pacific Northwest]] is an example of [[ecological release]] dependent on acclimation to colder winter temperatures, introduced plants, and human provision of nectar feeders during winter.

These birds feed on nectar from [[flower]]s using a long extendable tongue. They also consume small insects and other [[arthropod]]s caught in flight or gleaned from vegetation.

==Taxonomy==
Anna's hummingbird was [[species description|formally described]] and illustrated in 1829 by the French naturalist [[René Lesson]] in his ''Histoire naturelle des Oiseaux-Mouches'' from a specimen that had been collected in California. Lesson placed it in the [[genus]] ''Ornismya'' and coined the [[binomial name]] ''Ornismya anna''.<ref>{{ cite book | last=Lesson | first=René P. | author-link=René Lesson | year=1829 | title=Histoire naturelle des Oiseaux-Mouches | language=French | location=Paris | publisher=Arthus Bertrand  | page=[https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49416226 xxxi]; [https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/49416602 plate 74] }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1945 | title=Check-List of Birds of the World | volume=5 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=136 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14480147 }}</ref> Anna's hummingbird is now placed in the genus ''[[Calypte]]'' that was introduced in 1856 by the English ornithologist [[John Gould]].<ref>{{ cite book | last=Gould | first=John | author-link=John Gould | year=1856 | title=A Monograph of the Trochilidae, or Family of Humming-Birds | volume=4 | location=London | publisher=self | at=Plates 134, 135, 136 and text (Part 11, Plates 5, 6 and 7) | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/35246269 }} The 5 volumes were issued in 25 parts between 1849 and 1861. Title pages of all volumes bear the date of 1861.</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=August 2022 | title=Hummingbirds | work=IOC World Bird List Version 12.2 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/hummingbirds/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=17 September 2022 }}</ref> Gould did not explain the derivation of the genus name but it is probably from the [[Ancient Greek]] ''kaluptrē'' meaning "woman's veil" or "head-dress" (from ''kaluptō'' meaning "to cover"). The specific epithet ''anna'' was chosen to honour [[Anne d'Essling]] who married the ornithologist [[François Victor Massena, 3rd Duke of Rivoli]].<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | pages=[https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n86/mode/1up 86], [https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n48/mode/1up 48]}}</ref> The species is [[monotypic]]: no [[subspecies]] are recognised.<ref name=ioc/>

==Description==
Anna's hummingbirds are {{convert|3.9|to|4.3|in|cm|abbr=on}} long with a wingspan of {{convert|4.7|in|cm}} and a weight range of {{convert|0.1|to|0.2|oz|g|abbr=on}}.<ref name="cornell">{{cite web | title = Anna's Hummingbird | url =http://www.allaboutbirds.org/guide/annas_hummingbird/|publisher=Cornell University Laboratory of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY|date=2023| access-date =3 May 2023}}</ref> They have an [[Iridescence|iridescent]] bronze-green back, a pale grey chest and belly, and green flanks. Their bills are long, straight, and slender. The adult male has an iridescent [[crimson|crimson-red]], derived from magenta, to a reddish-pink crown and [[gorget (bird)|gorget]], which can look dull brown or gray without direct sunlight, and a dark, slightly forked tail. Females also have iridescent red gorgets, although they are usually smaller and less brilliant than the male.<ref name=cornell/>

[[File:AnnasHummingbirdPaloAltoNorvig.jpg|right|thumb|200px|A male bird displaying its iridescent head feathers]]
The male Anna's hummingbird is the only North American hummingbird species with a red [[crown (anatomy)|crown]].<ref name=cornell/><ref name="giraldo">{{cite journal | last1=Giraldo | first1=Marco A. | last2=Parra | first2=Juan L. | last3=Stavenga | first3=Doekele G. | title=Iridescent colouration of male Anna's hummingbird (''Calypte anna'') caused by multilayered barbules | journal=Journal of Comparative Physiology A: Neuroethology, Sensory, Neural & Behavioral Physiology | volume=204 | issue=12 | date=8 October 2018 | issn=0340-7594 | pmid=30298342 | pmc=6244987 | doi=10.1007/s00359-018-1295-8 | pages=965–975}}</ref> Females and juvenile males have a dull green crown, a grey throat with or without some red [[iridescence]], a grey chest and belly, and a dark, rounded tail with white tips on the outer feathers.<ref name=giraldo/>

The male has a striking reddish-pink crown and [[gorget]], which are strongly iridescent and dependent on the angle of illumination and observation by female or male competitor birds.<ref name=giraldo/> The iridescence results from large stacks of [[melanosome]]s in the feather [[Feather#Classification|barbules]], occurring as layers separated by [[keratin]].<ref name=giraldo/> The barbules reflect [[incident light]] in the manner of partially-opened [[Window blind#Venetian|Venetian blinds]], enabling the iridescence {{ndash}} which varies the head and gorget coloration with the changing angle of light {{ndash}} as a coloration advantage for courtship attraction and territory defense.<ref name=giraldo/>

Male birds with elevated levels of [[protein (nutrient)|protein]] in their diet have more colorful crowns and higher iridescence in their head feathers compared with male birds with low protein intake.<ref name="meadows">{{cite journal | last1=Meadows | first1=M. G. | last2=Roudybush | first2=T. E. | last3=McGraw | first3=K. J. | title=Dietary protein level affects iridescent coloration in Anna's hummingbirds, ''Calypte anna'' | journal=The Journal of Experimental Biology | volume=215 | issue=16 | date=25 July 2012 | issn=0022-0949 | pmid=22837446 | pmc=3404802 | doi=10.1242/jeb.069351 | pages=2742–2750}}</ref>

The male's call {{ndash}} scratchy and metallic {{ndash}} is typically used as the bird perches in trees and shrubs.<ref name=cornell/> Anna's hummingbirds have adapted to urban environments and are commonly seen in backyards and parks, and at feeders and flowering plants. Anna's hummingbirds eat flying insects.<ref name="nmf">{{cite web | title = Anna's hummingbird | url = http://naturemappingfoundation.org/natmap/facts/annas_hummingbird_712.html | date = 2021 | publisher = Washington NatureMapping Foundation|access-date=26 September 2021}}</ref>
[[File:Anna's Hummingbird, Orange County, CA, USA imported from iNaturalist photo 447453303.jpg|thumb|175px|Male displaying iridescent gorget]]

==Population statistics==
According to a 2021 estimate, there are 8 million Anna's hummingbirds in the western United States and Canada,<ref name="abc">{{cite web |first1=Kathryn |last1=Stonich |title=Hummingbirds of the United States: A Photo List of All Species |url=https://abcbirds.org/blog21/types-of-hummingbirds/ |publisher=American Bird Conservancy |access-date=7 March 2023 |date=26 April 2021}}</ref> with the population increasing since 1970.<ref name="English">{{cite journal | last1=English | first1=Simon G. | last2=Bishop | first2=Christine A. | last3=Wilson | first3=Scott | last4=Smith | first4=Adam C. | title=Current contrasting population trends among North American hummingbirds | journal=Scientific Reports | volume=11 | issue=1 | date=15 September 2021 | issn=2045-2322 | pmid=34526619 | pmc=8443710 | doi=10.1038/s41598-021-97889-x | page=18369| bibcode=2021NatSR..1118369E }}</ref> The global population is estimated to be 9.6 million mature individuals.<ref name="iucn" /> As of 2021, the [[IUCN Red List|International Union for Conservation of Nature Red List of Threatened Species]] lists Anna's hummingbird among [[least-concern species|species of least concern]].<ref name="iucn">{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International|title=''Calypte anna''|date=2021 |article-number=e.T22688199A186913595 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2021-3.RLTS.T22688199A186913595.en|publisher=International Union for Conservation of Nature}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
Anna's hummingbird is found along the western coast of North America, from southern Canada to northern Baja California, and inland to southern and central [[Arizona]], extreme southern [[Nevada]] and [[southeastern]] Utah, and western [[Texas]].<ref name=cornell/><ref name=Williamson>{{cite book |title=A Field Guide to Hummingbirds of North America |first=Sheri |last=Williamson |year=2001 |publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |location=New York, NY |isbn=0-618-02496-4 |page=199}}</ref> They tend to be permanent residents within their range. However, birds have been spotted far outside their range in such places as southern Alaska, [[Idaho]], Saskatchewan, New York, Florida, [[Louisiana]], and [[Newfoundland (island)|Newfoundland]].<ref>{{cite web|title=Unusual Hummingbird for Idaho: Anna's Hummingbird – Calypte anna |url=http://www.trochilids.com/Idaho/Annas/annas092004.html |access-date=2008-11-12 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20080516081136/http://www.trochilids.com/Idaho/Annas/annas092004.html |archive-date=2008-05-16 }}. See distribution map on bottom of page.</ref><ref>{{cite web | title = Pacific hummingbird found in eastern NFLD | url =https://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/newfoundland-labrador/pacific-hummingbird-found-in-eastern-nfld-1.1043086 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20121121235818/http://www.cbc.ca/news/canada/newfoundland-labrador/story/2011/01/24/nl-annas-hummingbird-124.html |archive-date=21 November 2012 | access-date = 10 November 2016 | work=CBC News | url-status = live | date=24 January 2011}}.</ref>

In response to rising temperatures at low elevations during [[climate change]] in the 21st century,  Anna's hummingbirds have expanded their range into the cooler summer environments of higher-altitude (up to {{convert|2825|m}}) mountainous terrains of California, such as the [[Sierra Nevada]].<ref name="spence">{{cite journal | last1=Spence | first1=Austin R. | last2=LeWinter | first2=Hannah | last3=Tingley | first3=Morgan W. | title=Anna's hummingbird (Calypte anna) physiological response to novel thermal and hypoxic conditions at high elevations | journal=Journal of Experimental Biology | volume=225 | issue=10 | date=2022-05-15 | issn=0022-0949 | doi=10.1242/jeb.243294 | pmid=35617822|url=https://journals.biologists.com/jeb/article/225/10/jeb243294/275376/Anna-s-hummingbird-Calypte-anna-physiological| doi-access=free }}</ref>

===Northern range expansion===
Anna's hummingbirds have the northernmost year-round range of any hummingbird. Birds have been recorded in Alaska as early as 1971, and resident in the [[Pacific Northwest]] since the 1960s, particularly increasing as a resident population during the early 21st century.<ref name=battey/><ref name=greig/> Scientists estimate that some birds overwinter and presumably breed at northern latitudes where food and shelter are available throughout winter, tolerating moderately cold winter temperatures.<ref name=battey/><ref name=greig/>

During cold temperatures, Anna's hummingbirds gradually gain weight during the day as they convert sugar to fat.<ref name=Beuchat>{{ cite journal |author1=Beuchat, C.A. |author2=Chaplin, S.B. |author3=Morton, M.L. |journal=Physiol. Zool.|pages=280–295|volume=52|issue=3 |year=1979|title=Ambient temperature and the daily energetics of two species of hummingbirds, ''Calypte anna'' and ''Selasphorus rufus''|doi=10.1086/physzool.52.3.30155751 |s2cid=87185364 }}</ref><ref name= Powers>{{ cite journal |author =Powers, D. R.|url=http://www.dpowerslab.com/wp-content/uploads/2011/09/PZ1991.pdf|jstor=30158211|title=Diurnal Variation in Mass, Metabolic Rate, and Respiratory Quotient in Anna's and Costa's Hummingbirds|journal=Physiological Zoology|volume=64|issue= 3 |year=1991|pages=850–870|doi=10.1086/physzool.64.3.30158211|s2cid=55730100}}</ref>

While their range was originally limited to the [[chaparral]] of California and [[Baja California Peninsula|Baja California]], it expanded northward to [[Oregon]], [[Washington (state)|Washington]], and [[British Columbia]], and east to [[Arizona]] in the 1960s and 70s.<ref name="battey">{{cite journal | last=Battey | first=C. J. | title=Ecological release of the Anna's hummingbird during a northern range expansion | journal=The American Naturalist| volume=194 | issue=3 | year=2019 | issn=0003-0147 | pmid=31553208 | doi=10.1086/704249 | pages=306–315| s2cid=164398193 | doi-access=free }}</ref> This rapid expansion is attributed to the widespread planting of [[flora|non-native species]], such as [[eucalyptus]], as well as the use of urban [[bird feeders]], in combination with the species' natural tendency for extensive postbreeding [[biological dispersal|dispersal]].<ref name=cornell/><ref>{{Cite news |last=Nuwer |first=Rachel |date=21 May 2025 |title=Bird feeders have caused a dramatic evolution of California hummingbirds |url=https://www.science.org/content/article/bird-feeders-have-caused-dramatic-evolution-california-hummingbirds}}</ref>

In the [[Pacific Northwest]], the fastest growing populations occur in regions with breeding-season cold temperatures similar to those of its native range.<ref name=battey/> Northward expansion of Anna's hummingbird represents an [[ecological release]] associated with introduced plants, year-round nectar availability from feeders supplied by humans, milder winter temperatures possibly associated with [[climate change]], and acclimation of the species to a winter climate cooler than its native region.<ref name=battey/><ref name="greig">{{cite journal | last1=Greig | first1=Emma I. | last2=Wood | first2=Eric M. | last3=Bonter | first3=David N. | title=Winter range expansion of a hummingbird is associated with urbanization and supplementary feeding | journal= Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences| volume=284 | issue=1852 | date=5 April 2017 | issn=0962-8452 | pmid=28381617 | pmc=5394677 | doi=10.1098/rspb.2017.0256 | article-number=20170256}}</ref> Although quantitative data are absent, it is likely that a sizable percentage of Anna's hummingbirds in the Pacific Northwest still do migrate south for winter, as of 2017.<ref name=greig/>
[[File:Anna's hummingbird in nocturnal torpor during winter in Vancouver, BC (cropped).jpg|thumb|Female in [[Nocturnality|nocturnal]] [[torpor]] during winter; {{convert|-8|C|F}}, near [[Vancouver]], British Columbia. The bird remained in torpor with an unchanged position for more than 12 hours.]]

===Torpor===
Anna's hummingbirds with inadequate stores of body fat or insufficient plumage are able to survive periods of subfreezing weather by lowering their metabolic rate and entering a state of [[torpor]].<ref name=spence/><ref>Russell, S.M. (1996). In ''The Birds of North America'', No. 226 (A. Poole and F. Gill, eds). The Academy of Natural Sciences, Philadelphia, and The American Ornithologists' Union, Washington DC</ref> 

When studied in colder temperatures at mountainous elevations, Anna's hummingbirds used torpor more frequently and for longer periods than at lower elevations.<ref name=spence/>

==Behavior==
===Foraging===
[[File:Hummmingbird at honeybee haven.webm|thumb|thumbtime=30|right|Feeding from ''[[Salvia]]'']]

This species consumes [[tree sap]], [[nectar]] from flowers, insects, and sugar-water mixes from [[Bird feeder|feeder]]s.<ref>{{cite book | last1 = Peterson | first1 = Roger Tory | last2 = Peterson | first2 = Virginia Marie | title = Peterson's Field Guide to Western Birds | place = Boston | publisher = Houghton Mifflin | year = 1990 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetowest00pete_1/page/216 216]–217 | isbn = 0-395-51424-X | url-access = registration | url = https://archive.org/details/fieldguidetowest00pete_1 }}</ref><ref name="adw">{{cite web|url=https://animaldiversity.org/accounts/Calypte_anna/|title=''Calypte anna'': Anna's hummingbird|publisher=Animal Diversity Web, University of Michigan, Museum of Zoology|date=2020|access-date=27 April 2024}}</ref> Anna's hummingbirds capture flying insects or eat insects trapped in [[spider web]]s, sometimes even the spiders themselves.<ref name=adw/>

===Pollination===
While collecting [[nectar]], Anna's hummingbird assists in plant [[pollination]].<ref name=English/> There is evidence that Anna's hummingbirds in flight generate an [[electrostatic charge]] that adheres [[pollen]] to their beaks and feathers, facilitating transfer of pollen grains to hundreds of flowers per day while foraging for nectar.<ref name="Badger">{{cite journal | last1=Badger | first1=Marc | last2=Ortega-Jimenez | first2=Victor Manuel | last3=von Rabenau | first3=Lisa | last4=Smiley | first4=Ashley | last5=Dudley | first5=Robert | editor-last=Gruverman | editor-first=Alexei | title=Electrostatic charge on flying hummingbirds and its potential role in pollination | journal=PLOS ONE| volume=10 | issue=9 | date=30 September 2015 | issn=1932-6203 | pmid=26421845 | pmc=4589311 | doi=10.1371/journal.pone.0138003 | article-number=e0138003| bibcode=2015PLoSO..1038003B | doi-access=free }}</ref> 

Anna's hummingbirds can shake their bodies 55 times per second to shed rain while in flight, or in dry weather, to remove pollen or dirt from feathers.<ref name=ANI42013>{{cite journal|journal= Journal of the Royal Society Interface|year=2012|volume=9|issue=70|pages=1093–1099|doi=10.1098/rsif.2011.0608|pmc=3306655|title=Aerial shaking performance of wet Anna's hummingbirds|last1=Ortega-Jimenez |first1=VM |last2=Dudley |first2=R |pmid=22072447}}</ref> Each twist lasts four-hundredths of a second and applies 34 times the force of gravity on the bird's head.<ref name=ANI42013/>

===Locomotion===
During hovering flight, Anna's hummingbirds maintain high wingbeat frequencies accomplished by their large [[thorax|pectoral muscle]]s via recruitment of [[motor unit]]s.<ref name="dla">{{Cite journal|last1=Altshuler|first1=Douglas L.|last2=Welch|first2=Kenneth C.|last3=Cho|first3=Brian H.|last4=Welch|first4=Danny B.|last5=Lin|first5=Amy F.|last6=Dickson|first6=William B.|last7=Dickinson|first7=Michael H.|date=15 July 2010|title=Neuromuscular control of wingbeat kinematics in Anna's hummingbirds (''Calypte anna'')|journal=Journal of Experimental Biology|language=en|volume=213|issue=14|pages=2507–2514|doi=10.1242/jeb.043497|issn=0022-0949|pmc=2892424|pmid=20581280}}</ref> The pectoral muscles that power hummingbird flight are composed exclusively of [[fast twitch muscle|fast]] [[glycolysis|glycolytic]] [[myofiber|fibers]] that respond rapidly and are fatigue-resistant.<ref name=dla/>

===Song and courtship===
[[File:Calypte anna - Anna's Hummingbird - XC109651.ogg|thumb|Song of a male Anna's hummingbird]]

Unlike most northern temperate hummingbirds, the male Anna's hummingbird sings during courtship. The song is thin and squeaky, interspersed with buzzes and chirps, and is drawn to over 10 seconds in duration. During the breeding season, males can be observed performing an aerial display dive over their territories. When a female flies onto a male's territory, the male rises up about {{convert|130|ft|m|abbr=on}} before diving over the female. As the male approaches the bottom of the dive, it reaches an average speed of {{convert|27|m/s|ft/s|abbr=on}}, which is 385 body lengths per second.<ref>{{cite journal| last = Clark | first = C.J. | journal = Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences| title=Courtship dives of Anna's hummingbird offer insights into flight performance limits | year=2009 | volume = 276 | issue = 1670 | pages = 3047–3052 | doi = 10.1098/rspb.2009.0508 | pmid=19515669 | pmc=2817121}}</ref> At the bottom of the dive, the male travels {{convert|23|m/s|mph|abbr=on}}, and produces an audible sound produced by the tail feathers, described by some as an "explosive squeak".<ref>{{cite journal| last1 = Clark | first1 = C.J. |journal= Proceedings of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences|title=The Anna's Hummingbird chirps with its tail: a new mechanism of sonation in birds|pmid=18230592 | year = 2008 | last2 = Feo | first2 = TJ | volume = 275 | issue = 1637 | pages = 955–62 | doi = 10.1098/rspb.2007.1619 | pmc = 2599939}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.sfgate.com/education/article/How-hummingbirds-chirp-It-s-all-in-the-tail-3228961.php |newspaper=San Francisco Chronicle |title=How hummingbirds chirp: It's all in the tail |last= Yollin |first= Patricia |date=8 February 2008|access-date=10 November 2016}}</ref>
[[File:Calypte anna nest (cropped).jpg|thumb|175px|Female nesting]]
[[File:Calypte anna feeding (cropped).jpg|thumb|175px|Female with two nestlings 12 days after hatch]]

===Breeding===
Open-[[wooded]] or [[shrub]]by areas and mountain meadows along the Pacific coast from [[British Columbia]] to [[Arizona]] make up ''C. anna''<!-- TO PREVENT BOLDING-->'s breeding habitat. The female raises the young without the assistance of the male. The female bird builds a nest in a shrub or tree, in vines, or attached to wires or other artificial substrates. The round, {{convert|3.8|to|5.1|cm|in|adj=on}} diameter nest is constructed of plant fibers, [[down feather|downy feathers]] and animal hair; the exterior is [[camouflage]]d with chips of [[lichen]], plant debris, and occasionally urban [[detritus]] such as paint chips and cigarette paper.<ref name=Williamson/> The nest materials are bound together with spider silk. They are known to nest as early as mid-December and as late as June, depending on geographic location and climatic conditions.<ref name=cornell/><ref>{{Cite book|title=A field guide to hummingbirds of North America|last=Sheri.|first=Williamson|date=2001|publisher=Houghton Mifflin|isbn=0-618-02495-6|location=Boston|oclc=46660593}}</ref>

==== Hybridization ====
Anna's hummingbirds may [[Hybrid (biology)|hybridize]] with other species, especially the [[genus|congeneric]] [[Costa's hummingbird]].<ref name=Williamson/> These natural hybrids have been mistaken for new species. A bird, allegedly collected in [[Bolaños (municipality)|Bolaños, Mexico]], was described and named ''Selasphorus floresii'' (Gould, 1861), or Floresi's hummingbird. Several more specimens were collected in California over a long period, and the species was considered extremely rare.<ref>{{ cite journal | last = Palmer | first = T.S. | title = Notes on persons whose names appear in the nomenclature of California birds | journal = [[Condor (journal)|The Condor]] | volume = 30 | issue = 5 | page = 277 | date = September 1928 | url = http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/condor/v030n05/p0261-p0307.pdf | doi=10.2307/1363227| jstor = 1363227 }}</ref>

The specimens were the hybrid offspring of an Anna's hummingbird and an [[Allen's hummingbird]]. A single bird collected in [[Santa Barbara, California]], was described and named ''Trochilus violajugulum'' (Jeffries, 1888), or violet-throated hummingbird.<ref>{{cite book | last = Ridgway | first = Robert | title = The Humming Birds | place = Washington DC | publisher = Smithsonian Institution | year = 1892 | pages = [https://archive.org/details/hummingbirds00ridgrich/page/331 331], 329 | url = https://archive.org/details/hummingbirds00ridgrich}}</ref> It was later determined to be a hybrid between an Anna's hummingbird and a [[black-chinned hummingbird]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last = Taylor | first = Walter P. | title = An instance of hybridization in hummingbirds, with remarks on the weight of generic characters in the Trochilidae | journal = [[Auk (journal)|The Auk]] | volume = 26 | issue = 3 | pages = 291–293 |year = 1909| doi = 10.2307/4070800 | jstor = 4070800 | url = https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/part/87277 }}</ref><ref>{{Cite journal | last = Ridgway | first = Robert | title = Hybridism and generic characters in the Trochilidae | journal = [[Auk (journal)|The Auk]] | volume = 26 | issue = 4 | pages = 440–442 | year = 1909 | url = http://sora.unm.edu/node/9006 | access-date = 11 November 2008 | doi = 10.2307/4071292 | jstor = 4071292 }}</ref>
[[File:Anna's Hummingbird, West Side, Vancouver, BC, Canada imported from iNaturalist photo 319360673.jpg|thumb|175px|Male in Vancouver]]

==Urbanization==

In the 2017 Vancouver Official City Bird Election, Anna's hummingbird was named the official bird of the city of [[Vancouver, British Columbia]].<ref>{{cite web|url=https://vancouver.ca/parks-recreation-culture/official-city-bird.aspx|title=Official City Bird: Anna's Hummingbird|publisher=City of Vancouver|date=2019|access-date=6 November 2019}}</ref> 

Anna's hummingbirds are non-migrating residents of [[Seattle]] where they live year-round through winter, enduring extended periods of subfreezing temperatures, snow, and high winds.<ref name="green">{{cite web |author1=Gregory A Green |title=Anna's Hummingbird: Our winter hummingbird |url=https://www.birdwatchingdaily.com/news/species-profiles/annas-hummingbird-our-winter-hummingbird/# |publisher=BirdWatching |access-date=6 November 2019 |date=2 October 2018}}</ref>

zh.extract:
朱红蜂鸟（学名：Calypte anna），也称安氏蜂鸟或紅喉蜂鳥，是雨燕目蜂鸟科的一种鸟类。
朱红蜂鸟体重约4.24克，翼长约49毫米，嘴峰长约20.6毫米，喙宽度约1.6毫米，喙厚度约1.7毫米，跗蹠长约4.6毫米，尾长约30毫米。朱红蜂鸟是部分迁徙的鸟类，其栖息在半开放的中等高度的林地中，食性为草食性，主要食物来源是植物的花蜜。
朱红蜂鸟分布于不列颠哥伦比亚省西南部至下加利福尼亚州西北部；越冬于墨西哥北部。该物种是单型物种，没有亚种分化。
